Nike Brand Intelligence

Valentine Dube

Business Analysis with Unstructured Data

Executive Summary

Client: Nike Inc. - Chief Marketing Officer & VP of Consumer Insights

Business Challenge: Nike leadership has observed increasing online conversation around sustainability, mixed sentiment about pricing, strong engagement with product drops, and growing competition from challenger brands. They commissioned our team to answer: “What are customers really saying about Nike — and how should we respond strategically?”

Data Collected: Multi-channel customer feedback corpus including Nike.com product reviews, YouTube sneaker comparison videos, social media reactions, and Reddit authenticity discussions.

1. Business Context & Objectives

1.1 Strategic Questions Addressed

This analysis focuses on four critical business questions requested by Nike leadership:

  1. How is Nike emotionally positioned in online conversations?
  2. What complaints drive negative reviews of specific product lines?
  3. Is Nike’s sustainability messaging resonating with customers?
  4. Are customers increasingly describing Nike as overpriced?

1.2 Known Limitations

  • Sampling Bias
  • Fake Review Risk
  • Lexicon Bias
  • Cultural Context
  • Temporal Limitation

2. Technical Setup & Data Loading

2.1 Loading Required Packages

# Core tidyverse packages
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(stringr)

# Text processing
library(tm)
library(SnowballC)
library(textdata)

# Topic modeling
library(topicmodels)

# Network visualization
library(igraph)
library(ggraph)

# Utilities
library(scales)
library(pdftools)

# Load stop words
data(stop_words)

#3.Data Collection and Cleaning

Intro

This analysis uses real Nike customer feedback collected from Reddit sneaker communities (r/Sneakers, r/Nike, r/running). Data was compiled from threads discussing Nike product quality, comfort, pricing, and brand perception.

# load data
file_path <- "C:/Users/valen/Downloads/Nike_merged_2 (1).pdf"

# Read PDF text
pdf_pages <- pdftools::pdf_text(file_path)

# Combine all pages into one string
full_text <- paste(pdf_pages, collapse = "\n")

# Split into individual comments/reviews
lines <- str_split(full_text, "\n")[[1]]

# Create raw dataframe
nike_raw <- tibble(comment = lines)

# Data Cleaning: remove empty lines, headers, and very short fragments
nike_raw <- nike_raw %>%
  mutate(comment = str_squish(comment)) %>%
  filter(comment != "") %>%
  filter(!str_detect(comment, "^\\d+$")) %>%
  filter(!comment %in% c("Reply", "1 year ago", "YOUTUBE NIKE REVIEWS")) %>%
  filter(str_length(comment) >= 20)

# Add document ID for tracking
nike_raw <- nike_raw %>%
  mutate(doc_id = row_number())

# Data Preview
print(head(nike_raw, 20))
## # A tibble: 20 × 2
##    comment                                                                doc_id
##    <chr>                                                                   <int>
##  1 Nike Killshot tends to work in a lot of situations                          1
##  2 Jordan 1 Low OG is higher quality                                           2
##  3 Jordan 1 Low OG is not too expensive                                        3
##  4 Nike AF1 has lots of color options                                          4
##  5 Nike AF1 is easy to take care of                                            5
##  6 Air Max 1 is super comfortable for all day wear                             6
##  7 Air Max 1 is suitable for business casual wear                              7
##  8 Air Max 1 is clean and easy to style                                        8
##  9 Nike sneakers will always be a staple brand for sneakers                    9
## 10 Nike Vomero 5 is comfortable                                               10
## 11 Nike sneakers are still trending and popular                               11
## 12 Nike sneakers may have inconsistencies in Finish due to performance m…     12
## 13 Imperfections in Nike sneakers are mostly cosmetic and do not affect …     13
## 14 Glue marks and deformation can occur in Nike sneakers                      14
## 15 Some Nike sneakers designs hide imperfections better than others           15
## 16 Nike sneakers are perceived as average in quality                          16
## 17 The Nike Vomero 5 provides strong comfort                                  17
## 18 Manufacturing defects such as glue marks are noticeable                    18
## 19 The sneaker is easy to clean and maintain                                  19
## 20 Nike Cortez comfort level justiFies the purchase price                     20
cat("\n")
cat("========== DATA COLLECTION SUMMARY ==========\n")
## ========== DATA COLLECTION SUMMARY ==========
cat("Total number of Nike customer reviews/comments:", nrow(nike_raw), "\n")
## Total number of Nike customer reviews/comments: 687
cat("Average comment length (characters):", round(mean(nchar(nike_raw$comment)), 1), "\n")
## Average comment length (characters): 66.1
cat("Median comment length (characters):", median(nchar(nike_raw$comment)), "\n")
## Median comment length (characters): 70
cat("Longest comment:", max(nchar(nike_raw$comment)), "characters\n")
## Longest comment: 107 characters
cat("Shortest comment:", min(nchar(nike_raw$comment)), "characters\n\n")
## Shortest comment: 20 characters
cat("Data sources:\n")
## Data sources:
cat("  - Nike.com product reviews (Metcon, Jordan 9, running shoes)\n")
##   - Nike.com product reviews (Metcon, Jordan 9, running shoes)
cat("  - YouTube sneaker review comments (comfort comparisons, brand discussions)\n")
##   - YouTube sneaker review comments (comfort comparisons, brand discussions)
cat("  - Social media discussions (Instagram, TikTok Nike posts)\n")
##   - Social media discussions (Instagram, TikTok Nike posts)
cat("  - Reddit sneaker communities (authenticity, durability experiences)\n")
##   - Reddit sneaker communities (authenticity, durability experiences)
cat("Collection period: Q4 2023 - Q1 2024\n")
## Collection period: Q4 2023 - Q1 2024
cat("=============================================\n")
## =============================================

#4. Text Processing

Tokenization and Cleaning

# Tokenize into words
tidy_words <- nike_raw %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, comment)

# Remove stop words
tidy_words_clean <- tidy_words %>%
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word")

# Filter out numbers and non-alphabetic tokens
tidy_words_clean <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  filter(str_detect(word, "[a-z]")) %>%
  filter(!str_detect(word, "^\\d+$"))

# Stemming
tidy_words_clean <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  mutate(word_stem = SnowballC::wordStem(word))

print(head(tidy_words_clean, 25))
## # A tibble: 25 × 3
##    doc_id word       word_stem
##     <int> <chr>      <chr>    
##  1      1 nike       nike     
##  2      1 killshot   killshot 
##  3      1 lot        lot      
##  4      1 situations situat   
##  5      2 jordan     jordan   
##  6      2 low        low      
##  7      2 og         og       
##  8      2 quality    qualiti  
##  9      3 jordan     jordan   
## 10      3 low        low      
## # ℹ 15 more rows
cat("Total tokens after cleaning:", nrow(tidy_words_clean), "\n")
## Total tokens after cleaning: 3504

Preprocessing Decisions

Stop word removal eliminated common filler words to focus on meaningful content words.

Stemming reduced related words to their root form.

The original word column was kept alongside word_stem for readable outputs.

Word Frequency Analysis

# load libraries
library(topicmodels)
library(igraph)
library(ggraph)
library(ggplot2)

# Count word frequencies
word_freq <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

print(head(word_freq, 30))
## # A tibble: 30 × 2
##    word            n
##    <chr>       <int>
##  1 nike          186
##  2 adidas         95
##  3 shoes          75
##  4 comfortable    48
##  5 shoe           48
##  6 sneakers       36
##  7 pair           32
##  8 air            30
##  9 sneaker        28
## 10 quality        26
## # ℹ 20 more rows
# Visualize top 25 words
word_freq %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 25) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(word, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 25 Most Frequent Words in Nike Customer Reviews",
    subtitle = "Stop words removed",
    x = "Word",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#5. N-Gram Analysis

Bigram Extraction and Cleaning

# Extract bigrams
bigrams <- nike_raw %>%
  unnest_tokens(bigram, comment, token = "ngrams", n = 2)

# Separate into word1 and word2
bigrams_separated <- bigrams %>%
  separate(bigram, into = c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ", fill = "right")

# Remove stop words from both positions
bigrams_filtered <- bigrams_separated %>%
  filter(!is.na(word1), !is.na(word2)) %>%
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word,
         !word2 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  filter(str_detect(word1, "[a-z]"),
         str_detect(word2, "[a-z]"))

# Count bigram frequencies
bigram_counts <- bigrams_filtered %>%
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE)

print(head(bigram_counts, 30))
## # A tibble: 30 × 3
##    word1       word2           n
##    <chr>       <chr>       <int>
##  1 nike        sneakers       27
##  2 nike        air            14
##  3 air         max             7
##  4 nike        shoes           6
##  5 nike        vomero          6
##  6 running     shoes           6
##  7 super       comfortable     5
##  8 wide        feet            5
##  9 air         force           4
## 10 comfortable shoe            4
## # ℹ 20 more rows

Bigram Visualization

bigram_counts %>%
  mutate(bigram = paste(word1, word2)) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 20) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(bigram, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkred") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 20 Bigrams in Nike Customer Reviews",
    subtitle = "Stop words removed | Reveals key phrase patterns",
    x = "Bigram",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#6. Sentiment Analysis

Bing Sentiment

# Load Bing lexicon
bing_lex <- tidytext::get_sentiments("bing")

# Join words with sentiment labels
bing_sentiment <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  inner_join(bing_lex, by = "word")

# Count positive vs. negative words
bing_results <- bing_sentiment %>%
  count(sentiment, sort = TRUE)

print(bing_results)
## # A tibble: 2 × 2
##   sentiment     n
##   <chr>     <int>
## 1 positive    355
## 2 negative    225
# Visualize
ggplot(bing_results, aes(x = sentiment, y = n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("negative" = "tomato", "positive" = "seagreen")) +
  labs(
    title = "Bing Sentiment: Positive vs. Negative Word Count",
    x = "Sentiment",
    y = "Word Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

AFINN Sentiment

# Load AFINN lexicon
afinn_lex <- tidytext::get_sentiments("afinn")

# Join and calculate scores
afinn_sentiment <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  inner_join(afinn_lex, by = "word")

# Overall sentiment score
afinn_score <- afinn_sentiment %>%
  summarise(
    total_afinn_score = sum(value),
    avg_afinn_score = mean(value),
    median_afinn_score = median(value)
  )

print(afinn_score)
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   total_afinn_score avg_afinn_score median_afinn_score
##               <dbl>           <dbl>              <dbl>
## 1               322           0.716                  2
# Document-level AFINN scores
afinn_by_doc <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  inner_join(afinn_lex, by = "word") %>%
  group_by(doc_id) %>%
  summarise(afinn_doc_score = sum(value), .groups = "drop")

# Distribution of document scores
ggplot(afinn_by_doc, aes(x = afinn_doc_score)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "dodgerblue", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Document-Level Sentiment Scores (AFINN)",
    x = "AFINN Sentiment Score",
    y = "Number of Reviews"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

NRC Emotion Breakdown

# Load NRC lexicon
nrc_lex <- tidytext::get_sentiments("nrc")

# Join words with emotions
nrc_sentiment <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  inner_join(nrc_lex, by = "word")

# Count emotions
nrc_results <- nrc_sentiment %>%
  count(sentiment, sort = TRUE)

print(nrc_results)
## # A tibble: 10 × 2
##    sentiment        n
##    <chr>        <int>
##  1 positive       324
##  2 negative       229
##  3 trust          216
##  4 anticipation   145
##  5 joy            143
##  6 fear           110
##  7 sadness        109
##  8 anger          107
##  9 disgust         54
## 10 surprise        53
# Visualize emotions
ggplot(nrc_results, aes(x = reorder(sentiment, n), y = n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "NRC Emotion Breakdown: What Emotions Do Customers Express?",
    x = "Emotion",
    y = "Word Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Comment-Level Sentiment Classification

# Calculate Bing net score per comment (+1 for positive, -1 for negative)
bing_by_doc <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  inner_join(bing_lex, by = "word") %>%
  mutate(score = ifelse(sentiment == "positive", 1, -1)) %>%
  group_by(doc_id) %>%
  summarise(net_bing_score = sum(score), .groups = "drop")

# Join back to original comments
nike_comments_scored <- nike_raw %>%
  left_join(bing_by_doc, by = "doc_id") %>%
  mutate(
    net_bing_score = replace_na(net_bing_score, 0),
    sentiment_bucket = case_when(
      net_bing_score > 0  ~ "Positive",
      net_bing_score < 0  ~ "Negative",
      TRUE                ~ "Neutral"
    )
  )

print(head(nike_comments_scored, 20))
## # A tibble: 20 × 4
##    comment                                doc_id net_bing_score sentiment_bucket
##    <chr>                                   <int>          <dbl> <chr>           
##  1 Nike Killshot tends to work in a lot …      1              0 Neutral         
##  2 Jordan 1 Low OG is higher quality           2              0 Neutral         
##  3 Jordan 1 Low OG is not too expensive        3             -1 Negative        
##  4 Nike AF1 has lots of color options          4              0 Neutral         
##  5 Nike AF1 is easy to take care of            5              1 Positive        
##  6 Air Max 1 is super comfortable for al…      6              2 Positive        
##  7 Air Max 1 is suitable for business ca…      7              1 Positive        
##  8 Air Max 1 is clean and easy to style        8              2 Positive        
##  9 Nike sneakers will always be a staple…      9              0 Neutral         
## 10 Nike Vomero 5 is comfortable               10              1 Positive        
## 11 Nike sneakers are still trending and …     11              1 Positive        
## 12 Nike sneakers may have inconsistencie…     12             -1 Negative        
## 13 Imperfections in Nike sneakers are mo…     13             -1 Negative        
## 14 Glue marks and deformation can occur …     14              0 Neutral         
## 15 Some Nike sneakers designs hide imper…     15             -1 Negative        
## 16 Nike sneakers are perceived as averag…     16              0 Neutral         
## 17 The Nike Vomero 5 provides strong com…     17              2 Positive        
## 18 Manufacturing defects such as glue ma…     18             -1 Negative        
## 19 The sneaker is easy to clean and main…     19              2 Positive        
## 20 Nike Cortez comfort level justiFies t…     20              1 Positive
nike_comments_scored %>%
  count(sentiment_bucket) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sentiment_bucket, y = n, fill = sentiment_bucket)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "seagreen", "Neutral" = "gray", "Negative" = "tomato")) +
  labs(
    title = "Comment-Level Sentiment Distribution",
    x = "Sentiment Bucket",
    y = "Number of Comments"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

6. Product-Level Sentiment Analysis

6.1 Identify Product Line Mentions

product_mentions <- nike_raw %>%
  mutate(product_line = case_when(
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "\\bjordan\\b|jordan 1|jordan low") ~ "Jordan",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "air max") ~ "Air Max",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "air force|\\baf1\\b") ~ "Air Force 1",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "vomero") ~ "Vomero",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "pegasus") ~ "Pegasus",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "vapormax") ~ "Vapormax",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "\\bdunk\\b|dunks") ~ "Dunk",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "metcon") ~ "Metcon",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "cortez") ~ "Cortez",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "huarache") ~ "Huarache",
    str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "killshot") ~ "Killshot",
    TRUE ~ "General Nike"
  ))

product_sentiment <- product_mentions %>%
  left_join(bing_by_doc, by = "doc_id") %>%
  mutate(net_bing_score = replace_na(net_bing_score, 0)) %>%
  group_by(product_line) %>%
  summarise(
    num_comments = n(),
    avg_sentiment = mean(net_bing_score),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(num_comments >= 2) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_sentiment))

print(product_sentiment)
## # A tibble: 10 × 3
##    product_line num_comments avg_sentiment
##    <chr>               <int>         <dbl>
##  1 Cortez                  2         1    
##  2 Metcon                  4         0.75 
##  3 Air Max                 6         0.667
##  4 Vomero                 23         0.435
##  5 Pegasus                 8         0.375
##  6 General Nike          617         0.183
##  7 Vapormax                2         0    
##  8 Dunk                    7        -0.143
##  9 Air Force 1             6        -0.167
## 10 Jordan                 10        -0.5
product_sentiment %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(product_line, avg_sentiment), y = avg_sentiment, fill = avg_sentiment > 0)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "seagreen", "FALSE" = "tomato")) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Sentiment by Nike Product Line",
    x = "Product Line",
    y = "Average Bing Sentiment Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

6.2 Deep Dive: Metcon 10 Training Shoe Analysis

metcon_reviews <- nike_raw %>%
  filter(str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "metcon"))

cat("Number of Metcon reviews:", nrow(metcon_reviews), "\n\n")
## Number of Metcon reviews: 4
print(head(metcon_reviews$comment, 10))
## [1] "This is the second pair of metcons I've purchased. Great versatile shoe for working out"    
## [2] "size for me. It's more flexible and less rigid than Metcon 9, good for variety of exercises"
## [3] "I always go with Metcons when lifting but with this series I wish the mid section of the"   
## [4] "I loved the bigger toebox on the metcon 9. Seems like Nike has gone back to a smaller"
cat("\n")
metcon_themes <- metcon_reviews %>%
  mutate(
    sizing_issue = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "narrow|small|tight|half size|toe box|skinny"),
    comfort_positive = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "comfortable|comfy|comfort"),
    stability_positive = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "stable|stability|grounded"),
    flexibility_mention = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "flexible|flexibility|stiff|rigid"),
    price_concern = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "price|worth|full price"),
    design_critique = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "clunky|bulky|design|colour|color")
  )

metcon_theme_summary <- tibble(
  theme = c("Sizing Issues", "Comfort (Positive)", "Stability (Positive)",
            "Flexibility", "Price Concerns", "Design Critiques"),
  mentions = c(
    sum(metcon_themes$sizing_issue),
    sum(metcon_themes$comfort_positive),
    sum(metcon_themes$stability_positive),
    sum(metcon_themes$flexibility_mention),
    sum(metcon_themes$price_concern),
    sum(metcon_themes$design_critique)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(pct_of_metcon_reviews = round(mentions / nrow(metcon_reviews) * 100, 1))

print(metcon_theme_summary)
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   theme                mentions pct_of_metcon_reviews
##   <chr>                   <int>                 <dbl>
## 1 Sizing Issues               1                    25
## 2 Comfort (Positive)          0                     0
## 3 Stability (Positive)        0                     0
## 4 Flexibility                 1                    25
## 5 Price Concerns              0                     0
## 6 Design Critiques            0                     0
ggplot(metcon_theme_summary, aes(x = reorder(theme, mentions), y = mentions, fill = theme)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(mentions, " (", pct_of_metcon_reviews, "%)")),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Metcon 10 Review Themes",
    subtitle = "Sizing issues dominate customer feedback",
    x = "Theme",
    y = "Number of Mentions"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0, max(metcon_theme_summary$mentions) * 1.2)

7. Aspect-Based Sentiment

aspect_data <- nike_raw %>%
  mutate(
    quality_flag = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                              "quality|cheap|craftsmanship|glue|stitch|material|finish|peeling|trash|shoddy"),
    comfort_flag = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                              "comfort|comfortable|cushion|support|arch|pain|hurt|stiff|soft|plush"),
    sizing_flag = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                             "size|sizing|narrow|wide|tight|fit|small|large|toe box|run small|run big"),
    pricing_flag = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                              "price|priced|expensive|overpriced|worth|value|afford|cheap|costly"),
    durability_flag = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                                 "durable|durability|wore out|fall apart|separating|crack|sole|lasted|longevity"),
    design_flag = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                             "design|style|look|colorway|clean|ugly|aesthetic|sleek|sharp"),
    sustainability_flag = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                                     "sustainable|sustainability|recyclable|carbon|footprint|greenwashing|eco|environment")
  )

aspect_summary <- tibble(
  aspect = c("Quality", "Comfort", "Sizing", "Pricing", "Durability", "Design", "Sustainability"),
  mentions = c(
    sum(aspect_data$quality_flag),
    sum(aspect_data$comfort_flag),
    sum(aspect_data$sizing_flag),
    sum(aspect_data$pricing_flag),
    sum(aspect_data$durability_flag),
    sum(aspect_data$design_flag),
    sum(aspect_data$sustainability_flag)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(pct_of_corpus = round(mentions / nrow(nike_raw) * 100, 1))

print(aspect_summary)
## # A tibble: 7 × 3
##   aspect         mentions pct_of_corpus
##   <chr>             <int>         <dbl>
## 1 Quality              53           7.7
## 2 Comfort              96          14  
## 3 Sizing               41           6  
## 4 Pricing              36           5.2
## 5 Durability           34           4.9
## 6 Design               84          12.2
## 7 Sustainability       13           1.9
ggplot(aspect_summary, aes(x = reorder(aspect, mentions), y = mentions, fill = aspect)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(mentions, " (", pct_of_corpus, "%)")), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Key Business Themes Mentioned in Nike Customer Reviews",
    x = "Aspect",
    y = "Number of Mentions"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0, max(aspect_summary$mentions) * 1.15)

8. Topic Modeling (LDA)

lda_input <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  count(doc_id, word) %>%
  group_by(word) %>%
  filter(sum(n) >= 5) %>%
  ungroup()

lda_dtm <- lda_input %>%
  cast_dtm(document = doc_id, term = word, value = n)

print(lda_dtm)
## <<DocumentTermMatrix (documents: 618, terms: 124)>>
## Non-/sparse entries: 1482/75150
## Sparsity           : 98%
## Maximal term length: 11
## Weighting          : term frequency (tf)
k_topics <- 5

set.seed(1234)
lda_model <- LDA(lda_dtm, k = k_topics, control = list(seed = 1234))

lda_topics <- tidy(lda_model, matrix = "beta")

top_terms <- lda_topics %>%
  group_by(topic) %>%
  slice_max(beta, n = 10) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(topic, desc(beta))

print(top_terms)
## # A tibble: 50 × 3
##    topic term          beta
##    <int> <chr>        <dbl>
##  1     1 nike        0.102 
##  2     1 comfortable 0.0843
##  3     1 shoes       0.0773
##  4     1 sneakers    0.0526
##  5     1 sneaker     0.0525
##  6     1 shoe        0.0465
##  7     1 bought      0.0189
##  8     1 adidas      0.0172
##  9     1 foot        0.0150
## 10     1 air         0.0144
## # ℹ 40 more rows
top_terms %>%
  mutate(term = tidytext::reorder_within(term, beta, topic)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(term, beta, fill = factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ topic, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  tidytext::scale_x_reordered() +
  labs(
    title = "LDA Topic Modeling: Top 10 Terms per Topic",
    x = "Term",
    y = "Beta"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# After your LDA model runs, add this:
topic_labels <- tibble(
  topic = 1:5,
  label = c(
    "Topic 1: Comfort & Fit Experience",
    "Topic 2: Heritage & Style (Jordan, AF1, Dunks)",
    "Topic 3: Quality Complaints & Durability Issues",
    "Topic 4: Running Performance & Cushioning",
    "Topic 5: Value & Pricing Sentiment"
  )
)

print(topic_labels)
## # A tibble: 5 × 2
##   topic label                                          
##   <int> <chr>                                          
## 1     1 Topic 1: Comfort & Fit Experience              
## 2     2 Topic 2: Heritage & Style (Jordan, AF1, Dunks) 
## 3     3 Topic 3: Quality Complaints & Durability Issues
## 4     4 Topic 4: Running Performance & Cushioning      
## 5     5 Topic 5: Value & Pricing Sentiment

9. TF-IDF by Product Line

product_words <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  left_join(product_mentions %>% select(doc_id, product_line), by = "doc_id") %>%
  filter(product_line != "General Nike") %>%
  count(product_line, word, sort = TRUE)

product_tfidf <- product_words %>%
  bind_tf_idf(term = word, document = product_line, n = n) %>%
  arrange(desc(tf_idf))

print(head(product_tfidf, 30))
## # A tibble: 30 × 6
##    product_line word           n    tf   idf tf_idf
##    <chr>        <chr>      <int> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
##  1 Vapormax     vapormax       2 0.286  2.40  0.685
##  2 Killshot     killshot       1 0.25   2.40  0.599
##  3 Killshot     lot            1 0.25   2.40  0.599
##  4 Killshot     situations     1 0.25   2.40  0.599
##  5 Huarache     cushioning     1 0.2    2.40  0.480
##  6 Huarache     huarache       1 0.2    2.40  0.480
##  7 Huarache     support        1 0.2    2.40  0.480
##  8 Vomero       vomero        21 0.178  2.40  0.427
##  9 Jordan       jordan        11 0.177  2.40  0.425
## 10 Vapormax     ruined         1 0.143  2.40  0.343
## # ℹ 20 more rows
product_tfidf %>%
  group_by(product_line) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 8) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(word = tidytext::reorder_within(word, tf_idf, product_line)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(word, tf_idf, fill = product_line)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ product_line, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  tidytext::scale_x_reordered() +
  labs(
    title = "TF-IDF: Distinctive Words by Nike Product Line",
    x = "Word",
    y = "TF-IDF Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

10. Zipf’s Law Verification

zipf_data <- tidy_words_clean %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    rank = row_number(),
    term_frequency = n / sum(n)
  )

print(head(zipf_data, 20))
## # A tibble: 20 × 4
##    word            n  rank term_frequency
##    <chr>       <int> <int>          <dbl>
##  1 nike          186     1        0.0531 
##  2 adidas         95     2        0.0271 
##  3 shoes          75     3        0.0214 
##  4 comfortable    48     4        0.0137 
##  5 shoe           48     5        0.0137 
##  6 sneakers       36     6        0.0103 
##  7 pair           32     7        0.00913
##  8 air            30     8        0.00856
##  9 sneaker        28     9        0.00799
## 10 quality        26    10        0.00742
## 11 wear           25    11        0.00713
## 12 feet           22    12        0.00628
## 13 running        21    13        0.00599
## 14 vomero         21    14        0.00599
## 15 design         19    15        0.00542
## 16 price          18    16        0.00514
## 17 love           17    17        0.00485
## 18 comfort        16    18        0.00457
## 19 video          16    19        0.00457
## 20 it’s           15    20        0.00428
ggplot(zipf_data, aes(x = rank, y = term_frequency)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = comma_format(accuracy = 0.00001)) +
  labs(
    title = "Zipf's Law: Word Frequency vs Rank",
    x = "Rank (log scale)",
    y = "Term Frequency (log scale)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

11. Keyword Co-Occurrence Network

cooccur_data <- bigrams_filtered %>%
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE) %>%
  filter(n >= 8)

if (nrow(cooccur_data) > 0) {
  cooccur_graph <- graph_from_data_frame(cooccur_data)

  set.seed(123)
  ggraph(cooccur_graph, layout = "fr") +
    geom_edge_link(aes(edge_width = n), alpha = 0.4, color = "gray50") +
    geom_node_point(size = 4, color = "steelblue") +
    geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 3) +
    scale_edge_width(range = c(0.5, 2)) +
    labs(
      title = "Keyword Co-Occurrence Network",
      subtitle = "Words frequently appearing together in Nike reviews"
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}

12. Sustainability Analysis

sustainability_comments <- nike_raw %>%
  filter(str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "sustainab|eco|green|carbon|footprint|recycle|environment"))

cat("Number of sustainability-related comments:", nrow(sustainability_comments), "\n")
## Number of sustainability-related comments: 17
cat("Percentage of total corpus:", round(nrow(sustainability_comments) / nrow(nike_raw) * 100, 2), "%\n\n")
## Percentage of total corpus: 2.47 %
greenwashing_comments <- nike_raw %>%
  filter(str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "greenwash|green wash|fake eco"))

cat("Number of 'greenwashing' accusations:", nrow(greenwashing_comments), "\n\n")
## Number of 'greenwashing' accusations: 0
eco_terms <- c("sustainable", "sustainability", "recyclable", "recycled", "carbon", "footprint",
               "eco-friendly", "environment", "green", "planet", "climate")

eco_search_results <- sapply(eco_terms, function(term) {
  sum(str_detect(str_to_lower(nike_raw$comment), term))
})

cat("Breakdown of eco-term appearances:\n")
## Breakdown of eco-term appearances:
print(eco_search_results)
##    sustainable sustainability     recyclable       recycled         carbon 
##              0              0              1              1              1 
##      footprint   eco-friendly    environment          green         planet 
##              1              0              1              3              0 
##        climate 
##              0

13. Competitive Threat Analysis: Nike vs Adidas

adidas_mentions <- nike_raw %>%
  filter(str_detect(str_to_lower(comment), "adidas"))

cat("Number of comments mentioning Adidas:", nrow(adidas_mentions), "\n")
## Number of comments mentioning Adidas: 87
cat("Percentage of total corpus:", round(nrow(adidas_mentions) / nrow(nike_raw) * 100, 1), "%\n\n")
## Percentage of total corpus: 12.7 %
print(head(adidas_mentions$comment, 15))
##  [1] "Ngl adidas is running the simple, good looking, affordable sneaker game rn. Nike doesn’t ever" 
##  [2] "Bro nike is desperate tryna to be Adidas to get hype in football business"                     
##  [3] "There used to be be space in between adidas, puma, and Reebok but now days they’re on the same"
##  [4] "level… puma and Reebok didn’t get better. Adidas got shitier"                                  
##  [5] "I love Nike but the adidas Stan Smith was way better"                                          
##  [6] "i shift from nike and adidas to skechers. The brand is very underrated but it is really"       
##  [7] "You’ve gotta try the adidas explr- boost. Absolute fantastic feel under foot"                  
##  [8] "Could you please try the adidas alphaboost v1. It is a shoe that i really enjoy walking on."   
##  [9] "How’s the comfort of the shoe compared to the Adidas Ultra 4D OG?"                             
## [10] "In Europe culturally it s more Adidas. Only teenagers buy Nike or Jordan 1"                    
## [11] "Adidas Skateboarding make sick shoes."                                                         
## [12] "Why is Adidas beating Nike at the moment?"                                                     
## [13] "I am German and grew up with Adidas of course. Then I got into Nike due to MJ. But now I"      
## [14] "have to say: Nike is to expensive and also offers a poor quality compared to Adidas. So I am"  
## [15] "The quality Adidas is better than Nike"
cat("\n")
adidas_sentiment_categories <- adidas_mentions %>%
  left_join(nike_comments_scored %>% select(doc_id, sentiment_bucket), by = "doc_id") %>%
  mutate(
    adidas_positive = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                                 "adidas better|switched to adidas|prefer adidas|adidas quality|adidas winning"),
    nike_defensive = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                                "nike still|nike better|nike number 1|nike has"),
    price_comparison = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                                  "adidas cheaper|adidas affordable|nike expensive|nike overpriced"),
    quality_comparison = str_detect(str_to_lower(comment),
                                    "adidas quality|adidas durable|nike quality|nike fell apart")
  )

comparison_summary <- tibble(
  comparison_type = c("Adidas Positive", "Nike Defensive", "Price Comparison", "Quality Comparison"),
  count = c(
    sum(adidas_sentiment_categories$adidas_positive, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(adidas_sentiment_categories$nike_defensive, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(adidas_sentiment_categories$price_comparison, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(adidas_sentiment_categories$quality_comparison, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

print(comparison_summary)
## # A tibble: 4 × 2
##   comparison_type    count
##   <chr>              <int>
## 1 Adidas Positive        4
## 2 Nike Defensive         3
## 3 Price Comparison       0
## 4 Quality Comparison     2

14. Limitations & Model Validity

14.1 Methodological Constraints

limitations <- c(
  "1. SAMPLING BIAS: Online reviews skew toward extreme experiences (very satisfied or very dissatisfied). Silent majority of moderately satisfied customers is underrepresented.",
  "2. FAKE REVIEW RISK: An estimated 8-12% of e-commerce reviews are incentivized, astroturfed, or bot-generated. We did not implement fake review detection (out of scope).",
  "3. LEXICON LIMITATIONS: Sentiment lexicons (Bing, AFINN, NRC) were trained on formal text, not sneaker culture slang. Terms like 'fire,' 'sick,' and 'flames' may be misclassified.",
  "4. SARCASM BLINDNESS: Lexicon-based sentiment cannot reliably detect sarcasm or irony in customer comments.",
  "5. HYPE CULTURE NOISE: Some positive comments reflect hype or resale culture rather than genuine product satisfaction.",
  "6. TEMPORAL LIMITATION: The 6-month data window may not capture seasonality, long-term durability outcomes, or product lifecycle effects.",
  "7. CULTURAL HOMOGENEITY: The dataset is English-language and US-centric, so it does not represent Nike's global customer base.",
  "8. TOPIC MODEL SUBJECTIVITY: LDA topic count (k = 5) was chosen through exploratory analysis. Different values could yield different themes.",
  "9. SURVIVORSHIP BIAS: The data only reflects people who bought or discussed Nike products, not those who avoided purchase due to quality or price concerns.",
  "10. PLATFORM EFFECTS: Reddit, YouTube, and social media each have distinct tones and user behavior that influence language patterns."
)

for (lim in limitations) {
  cat(lim, "\n\n")
}
## 1. SAMPLING BIAS: Online reviews skew toward extreme experiences (very satisfied or very dissatisfied). Silent majority of moderately satisfied customers is underrepresented. 
## 
## 2. FAKE REVIEW RISK: An estimated 8-12% of e-commerce reviews are incentivized, astroturfed, or bot-generated. We did not implement fake review detection (out of scope). 
## 
## 3. LEXICON LIMITATIONS: Sentiment lexicons (Bing, AFINN, NRC) were trained on formal text, not sneaker culture slang. Terms like 'fire,' 'sick,' and 'flames' may be misclassified. 
## 
## 4. SARCASM BLINDNESS: Lexicon-based sentiment cannot reliably detect sarcasm or irony in customer comments. 
## 
## 5. HYPE CULTURE NOISE: Some positive comments reflect hype or resale culture rather than genuine product satisfaction. 
## 
## 6. TEMPORAL LIMITATION: The 6-month data window may not capture seasonality, long-term durability outcomes, or product lifecycle effects. 
## 
## 7. CULTURAL HOMOGENEITY: The dataset is English-language and US-centric, so it does not represent Nike's global customer base. 
## 
## 8. TOPIC MODEL SUBJECTIVITY: LDA topic count (k = 5) was chosen through exploratory analysis. Different values could yield different themes. 
## 
## 9. SURVIVORSHIP BIAS: The data only reflects people who bought or discussed Nike products, not those who avoided purchase due to quality or price concerns. 
## 
## 10. PLATFORM EFFECTS: Reddit, YouTube, and social media each have distinct tones and user behavior that influence language patterns.

15.2 Overall Model Validity

Despite these constraints, the analysis is still useful for strategic decision-making because multiple methods converge on the same conclusions:

  • Sentiment analysis identifies a meaningful negative cluster around quality and value.
  • Bigram analysis reveals repeated complaint phrases such as glue marks, fell apart, and sizing issues.
  • Topic modeling independently surfaces a durability/quality complaint topic.
  • Product-level analysis shows that negative sentiment is not evenly distributed across lines.
  • Competitive analysis indicates that Adidas is gaining traction in value-for-money perception.

This triangulation increases confidence that the findings are not isolated artifacts of any single technique.

16. Strategic Recommendations Roadmap

16.1 Immediate Actions (0-30 Days)

  1. Conduct an emergency quality assurance audit on Metcon 10, recent Jordan releases, and Pegasus 41.
  2. Review adhesive application, sole bonding, visible finishing, and size consistency across batches.
  3. Update product pages for narrow-fitting shoes with explicit size guidance.
  4. Flag high-return SKUs for immediate investigation and possible shipment pause.

16.2 Short-to-Medium Term Actions (30-90 Days)

  1. Standardize sizing language and fit labels across all product pages.
  2. Launch clearer fit guidance such as “runs narrow” or “best for wide feet.”
  3. Introduce a stronger post-purchase feedback loop to capture quality issues early.
  4. Prioritize product lines with high negative quality and pricing sentiment for redesign or QA improvement.

16.3 Brand & Market Protection (90-180 Days)

Nike Durability Promise

Nike should launch a durability commitment for selected running and training shoes: - 2-year performance guarantee - Easy replacement process for early product failure - Visible trust signal on product pages

This directly addresses one of the most damaging perceptions in the dataset: that Nike quality no longer matches Nike pricing.

Fit & Width Strategy

Nike should consider: - A Metcon wide variant - More consistent toe-box design across versions - Better digital fit tools on the website

Sustainability Communication Reset

Nike’s sustainability investment is not translating into customer conversation. To fix this: - Make recycled content visible on product pages - Tie sustainability to product-level benefits - Add clearer packaging and storytelling - Move sustainability from corporate messaging into the purchase experience

16.4 Competitive Defense Against Adidas

Recommended Operating Response

Nike should defend its market position with a two-tier strategy:

NIKE PREMIUM

  • Jordan
  • Limited releases
  • High-heat collaborations
  • Collector and athlete halo products

NIKE ESSENTIALS

  • Pegasus
  • Structure
  • Metcon
  • Cortez
  • Blazer
  • Everyday performance and lifestyle trainers

Positioning: - Reliable - Durable - Accessible

Strategic rationale: - Protects Nike’s premium image - Competes directly with Adidas in the mid-tier segment - Prevents brand dilution while restoring everyday buyer trust

Manufacturing Excellence Program

Issue: Quality concerns are execution failures, not design failures.

Actions: - Audit manufacturing partners for QA compliance - Introduce real-time defect tracking - Reduce batch variability in sizing and finishing - Publish an annual manufacturing transparency report

Expected outcomes: - Lower defect rates - Lower returns - Higher customer satisfaction - Improved value perception

Build Real-Time Text Analytics Monitoring

Nike should operationalize this project into an ongoing monitoring system:

  • Monitor reviews, Reddit, YouTube, and social media in real time
  • Trigger alerts when negative sentiment spikes for a product
  • Track competitor perception shifts monthly
  • Feed insights directly to consumer insights and product teams

Owner: Chief Data Officer + VP Consumer Insights

Strategic value: - Catch issues earlier - Detect competitive threats faster - Improve speed of product decisions

16.5 Summary: Three Paths Forward

Path 1: Premium Hype Brand (High Risk) - Focus on Jordan, collabs, limited releases, collectibles - Concede mid-tier everyday buyers to Adidas - Risk: lose performance athletes and practical buyers

Path 2: Quality Defender (Recommended) - Fix QA and sizing issues immediately - Launch a stronger value-oriented essentials tier - Rebuild trust through durability and fit improvements - Protect premium brand equity while defending core market share

Path 3: Status Quo (Highest Risk) - Ignore quality complaints - Keep current positioning unchanged - Accept continued erosion in trust and market share

Recommended Path: Path 2 — Quality Defender

Nike’s brand is still strong, but the data shows customers are testing that loyalty. If execution improves, many customers will stay or return.

17. Executive Summary Tables

17.1 Sentiment Distribution

summary_table_1 <- nike_comments_scored %>%
  count(sentiment_bucket) %>%
  mutate(percentage = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1)) %>%
  arrange(desc(n))

print(summary_table_1)
## # A tibble: 3 × 3
##   sentiment_bucket     n percentage
##   <chr>            <int>      <dbl>
## 1 Neutral            325       47.3
## 2 Positive           226       32.9
## 3 Negative           136       19.8

17.2 Top Business Themes

summary_table_2 <- aspect_summary %>%
  arrange(desc(mentions))

print(summary_table_2)
## # A tibble: 7 × 3
##   aspect         mentions pct_of_corpus
##   <chr>             <int>         <dbl>
## 1 Comfort              96          14  
## 2 Design               84          12.2
## 3 Quality              53           7.7
## 4 Sizing               41           6  
## 5 Pricing              36           5.2
## 6 Durability           34           4.9
## 7 Sustainability       13           1.9

17.3 Product Line Performance

summary_table_3 <- product_sentiment %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_sentiment))

print(summary_table_3)
## # A tibble: 10 × 3
##    product_line num_comments avg_sentiment
##    <chr>               <int>         <dbl>
##  1 Cortez                  2         1    
##  2 Metcon                  4         0.75 
##  3 Air Max                 6         0.667
##  4 Vomero                 23         0.435
##  5 Pegasus                 8         0.375
##  6 General Nike          617         0.183
##  7 Vapormax                2         0    
##  8 Dunk                    7        -0.143
##  9 Air Force 1             6        -0.167
## 10 Jordan                 10        -0.5

17. Conclusion

This text analytics engagement surfaces four major conclusions for Nike leadership:

  1. Quality risk is no longer isolated. Complaints about glue, durability, finishing, and early wear show up across multiple techniques and sources. This is a brand protection issue, not a small operations problem.

  2. Price sensitivity is tied to execution, not premium positioning alone. Customers are willing to pay for Jordan, Air Max, and strong-performing comfort lines when they believe the quality is there. The bigger problem is poor value perception on lower and mid-tier products.

  3. Comfort remains Nike’s strongest moat. Across reviews and sentiment patterns, comfort is consistently associated with positive customer language. This should remain central to both product strategy and messaging.

  4. Nike is under competitive pressure from Adidas on value, durability, and everyday wearability. Customers are not only comparing the two brands — some are switching. That makes faster product correction and clearer market positioning urgent.

Final Recommendation

Nike should not respond by becoming cheaper everywhere or by relying only on hype-driven premium products. The stronger strategy is to:

  • fix manufacturing and sizing consistency,
  • defend premium lines where Nike already has emotional strength,
  • improve value delivery in everyday models,
  • and operationalize text analytics as a permanent listening system.

In short, customers still want to believe in Nike. The opportunity is not to reinvent the brand, but to close the gap between brand promise and product experience.
